item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A SCP designation shouldn't be in a footnote. The text was much harder to follow as a result.

I'll also second the "they." In the nineties in the U.S., "they" would have been more often seen in speculative fiction refering to neutral sapient entities than with persons not conforming to establishment gender roles. Given that clowns can be construed as alien, that is what I took it as, initially. And although dramatic ambiguity is a sought-after trope on this site, the fuzziness at the end didn't sit well with me. I don't think that a scientist would treat noncorporeal sapience with the kind of easily satisfied disinterest displayed here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The definition of what 4459A is would look better in the main text, it doesn't need to be a footnote. If you can just move your mouse over the "1" to read the note it doesn't matter, but that doesn't work for everyone (for me, it works on old-format articles but not new-format ones), in which case it'd save scrolling down to the bottom and losing the thread.

Nice story, thanks. It's funny, it's a vivid little snapshot of daily anomalous life at the Circus, but the point of the story is barely even about an anomaly, except for the basic scenario of one of them having a longer life expectancy than the other. It's just a story about two acrobats who fell in love and the complicated life they led. I'm glad that Doctor Stewart managed to get the old ghost his mementos back without getting hauled away on suspicion of having been brainwashed, which seems to be an ever-present hazard for Researchers who try to be kind to their subjects!

I'm not crazy about the use of a (completely human) "they". It seems shoehorned in, given that in this setting it's an anachronism - the first bit of Fraust's diary is supposed to have been written at least 23 years ago when the idea of "non-binary" hadn't really been invented. So it makes the story seem less convincing. Not that Cahaya mightn't well have been a person who today would have liked to be considered as "non-binary", but they wouldn't have used that terminology then, certainly not as if it was a well-known thing that required no explanation.

It's not totally impossible, I suppose - this is the Circus, perhaps this was a concept among Clowns, or in one of the weird dimensions they'd been on tour in, before it was so among regular humans. After all, they have some strange creatures working for them. Perhaps they have some performers who they'd been in the habit of calling "they" because they objectively aren't men or women, and Cahaya - or some other gender-dissatisfied human performer before her - got the idea from them and decided he/she wanted to be a they too.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm so glad that's what you got from this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Haha… Pi-6, "Clown Wranglers." I get it. Pi = Pie… Haahaaa….HAHAHA!!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This was sweetly, sadly beautiful. It showed a side of the Circus we rarely see.

item.title: Author Post
item.content:encoded:
This wouldn't have happened without my wonderful critters Doctor Fullham and SoullessSingularity. Thank you so very much for keeping me on task.
